sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient presents with a sore on the right foot that has not healed for 2-3 weeks.
The patient reports that the wound was caused by a blister from a new pair of shoes, which was initially not felt due to neuropathy.
The patient has been putting a band-aid on the wound and has completed a course of antibiotics prescribed by their primary care physician (PCP) yesterday, but the wound has not improved.
The patient denies any pain, fever, chills, or drainage from the wound.
The patient reports that the wound sticks to their sock when they remove it.
Past medical history: The patient has diabetes, with an A1c level of over 8.
The patient has a history of seeing a podiatrist 2 years ago, but the podiatrist moved and the patient was unable to find a new one.
Review of systems: The patient denies any other symptoms.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's temperature is 98.1°F, heart rate is 72, respirations are 16, and blood pressure is 110/65.
Physical exam: The patient has a 1x2 inch circular wound on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot, proximal to the right 5th MTP joint, with yellow slough and minimal granulation tissue.
There is no surrounding erythema or cellulitis, no fluid collection, no necrosis, and no odor.
The patient has palpable bilateral femoral and popliteal pulses, but no palpable DP or PT pulses, with Doppler signs present.
Test Results: The patient's right foot X-ray shows no evidence of osteomyelitis.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient presents with a diabetic foot ulcer that requires further evaluation and management.
Problem list: Diabetic foot ulcer; Poor wound healing; Diabetes mellitus; Neuropathy.
Plan:
I ordered an Ankle Brachial Index (ABI) to determine the blood supply to the foot.
I performed a debridement in the office to remove dead tissue from the wound.
I prescribed collagenase ointment to be applied to the wound once daily, covered with a dry sterile dressing.
The patient will wear a surgical shoe to take pressure off the area.
I do not think any more antibiotics are needed at this time.
The patient will return in 2 weeks for follow-up.
Follow-up: The patient will return in 2 weeks for follow-up.
The patient may need to be referred to a vascular specialist if the ABI indicates poor blood supply to the foot.
The patient may also need a diabetic consult to discuss ways to manage their diabetes and lower their A1c level.
